thread.text:
anyone seen this? serving Llama-3-70B on 2x A100 and it dies on startup:
```torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 1.94 GiB (GPU 0; 79.20 GiB total capacity; 76.34 GiB already allocated)```
gpu_memory_utilization is default (0.9). single GPU with the 8B model works fine. using tensor_parallel_size=2

classic TP2 footgun — with tensor_parallel_size=2, the KV cache math changes but the default gpu_memory_utilization=0.9 still tries to reserve 90% *per GPU* for the cache. drop it to 0.85 and set --max-model-len explicitly (e.g. 4096). the allocator is reserving capacity for max context length you'll never use
that was it :pray: --max-model-len 4096 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.85 and it's up. wild that the OOM message doesn't give a hint about this. adding to our runbook

fresh install on clean venv, `pip install vllm` succeeds but import explodes:
```ImportError: /home/user/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/vllm/_C.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so: undefined symbol: _ZN3c104cuda20CUDACachingAllocator6Config...```
torch 2.3.0, CUDA 12.1 driver, fresh ubuntu 22.04. what am I missing

that symbol error is almost always a torch/vllm CUDA build mismatch — the vllm wheel was built against a different version of torch than what you have installed. solution: nuke the venv, install vllm FIRST and let pip pull its pinned torch dependency, do NOT pre-install torch yourself

```
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install vllm
```

if you need a specific torch version for other reasons, check the vllm release notes for the compatible torch pin
confirmed working! the pre-installed torch 2.3.0 was conflicting. vllm pulled torch 2.2.1 and everything is fine now. wish the error message said something about torch version
yeah this bites a lot of people. the symbol names encode the ABI and when torch changes internals between versions the .so won't link. same fix every time

trying to serve mistral-7b-instruct-v0.2 with awq quantization:
```ValueError: The model's max seq len (32768) is larger than the maximum number of tokens that can be stored in KV cache (11264). Try increasing `gpu_memory_utilization` or decreasing `max_model_len` when initializing the engine.```
i'm on a single 24GB GPU (RTX 3090). using `quantization='awq'` in the engine args

AWQ quantization reduces model weights size but the KV cache is still fp16 and takes the same memory. for a 24GB card with a 32k context model you need to either: (1) set --max-model-len 8192 or lower to cap context, or (2) increase gpu_memory_utilization to 0.95 (risky on 24GB). option 1 is the right call for most use cases — 8k is plenty for chat
--max-model-len 8192 worked perfectly. serving at >200 tok/s now. thanks!

getting intermittent 500s from the openai-compatible endpoint under load. single A100, running mixtral 8x7b. errors look like:
```Exception in ASGI application
vllm.engine.async_llm_engine: Engine background task failed
ValueError: Request ... not found```
anyone else seeing this?

this is a known race condition when requests time out on the client side but the engine hasn't cleaned them up yet — the request ID disappears from the tracker mid-flight. workaround: set a longer client timeout so the client doesn't cancel before vllm finishes. which client are you using?
using python requests with timeout=30. the model latency is sometimes 40s on long outputs
there it is — bump your client timeout to 120s+ and you should stop seeing it. also consider streaming the response so the client gets tokens before the full generation completes
switching to streaming fixed it. timeouts are gone. should have done that from the start honestly

does vllm support Falcon-180B? trying to load it and getting:
```ValueError: Model architectures ['FalconForCausalLM'] are not supported for now.```
on vllm 0.2.7
Falcon-180B wasn't in 0.2.7 but was added in 0.3.0. `pip install --upgrade vllm` should get you there. also you'll need at least 4x A100 80GB for it — that model is ~360GB in bf16
upgraded to 0.3.2 and it loads now. we have 8x H100s so memory is fine. appreciate the quick answer
how do i enable speculative decoding in vllm? i see it in the docs but the --speculative-model flag gives `error: unrecognized arguments`
spec decoding was added in 0.4.1 and the flag is `--num-speculative-tokens` along with `--speculative-model`. what version are you on?
0.3.3 :facepalm: upgrading now
yeah 0.4.x is worth the upgrade for spec decoding alone. you can see 2-3x throughput on chat workloads with a good draft model

seeing very high first-token latency (TTFT) when serving with continuous batching — sometimes 8-10s even for simple prompts. throughput looks fine but latency is rough for interactive use. any tuning tips?

continuous batching prioritizes throughput over latency by design — long sequences in the batch block short ones from starting. try:
1. `--max-num-seqs 32` (lower batch cap)
2. `--max-num-batched-tokens 2048` (caps how much prefill can happen per step)
3. if you can afford it, a dedicated low-latency endpoint with small max-num-seqs and a throughput endpoint separately
max-num-seqs 32 made a huge difference, TTFT dropped to ~1s. the tradeoff is real but worth it for our use case. thanks!

multi-GPU question: we have 2x A6000 (48GB each) and want to serve llama-2-70b. tensor_parallel_size=2 fails at init:
```torch.distributed.DistBackendError: NCCL error in: ../torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/ProcessGroupNCCL.cpp:1275, unhandled system error, NCCL version 2.18.3```
both GPUs visible via nvidia-smi

NCCL needs peer-to-peer access between the GPUs. check `nvidia-smi topo -m` — you want NV2 or NV4 between the two cards (nvlink). if you see PIX (PCIe) you'll need to enable P2P explicitly or NCCL will struggle. also try setting `NCCL_P2P_DISABLE=1` as a diagnostic to see if P2P is the issue
`nvidia-smi topo -m` shows PIX between them (different PCIe roots). NCCL_P2P_DISABLE=1 lets it start but it's slow. we ended up getting a PCIe switch which helped. not ideal

PCIe topology is a real constraint for multi-GPU inference. for A6000s without NVLink, pipeline parallelism can sometimes be more efficient than tensor parallelism — fewer cross-device all-reduces. worth trying `pipeline_parallel_size=2, tensor_parallel_size=1` and comparing throughput
can vllm serve multiple models at the same time on the same GPU? like two 7B models?

not natively — vllm allocates all available GPU memory to a single model at startup. you'd need to run two separate vllm processes with explicit CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to partition the GPU... which only works if each model fits in half the VRAM. for a 24GB card and two 7B models that's tight but possible (7B fp16 = ~14GB, quantized to ~4-5GB each is doable)
so something like CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 vllm serve model-a --port 8000 and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 vllm serve model-b --port 8001 but I'm guessing they'll fight over memory?

exactly — set `--gpu-memory-utilization 0.45` on each process and they'll each take ~half the card. fragile but it works for light workloads. a proper solution is model multiplexing but that's not in vllm yet
getting weird outputs from Llama-3-8B-Instruct — it ignores the system prompt and just continues generating the user message verbatim. same model on ollama works fine

are you using the chat completions endpoint or the raw completions endpoint? for instruction-tuned models you need `/v1/chat/completions` with the messages array — if you call `/v1/completions` the chat template isn't applied and the model sees raw text, not the formatted prompt
I was using /v1/completions :facepalm: switched to /v1/chat/completions and it's behaving correctly now. the distinction makes sense in retrospect
is there a way to get vllm to return token probabilities / logprobs? building a calibration tool and need them
yes — in the completions API set `logprobs=5` (returns top-5 logprobs per token). for the chat completions API it's `logprobs=true` + `top_logprobs=5`. added in 0.4.0 I think
perfect, that's exactly what I needed. the openai api compat is really nice for this
heads up: vLLM 0.5.0 just dropped with PagedAttention v2 and fp8 kv cache support. big throughput improvements on H100s. release notes in the usual place
already on it. fp8 kv cache is a game changer for long context — 2x the effective context window at same memory cost
does it break existing deployments? we're pinned to 0.4.3
no breaking changes on the API surface. engine args added a few new optional flags but existing configs work unchanged
:rocket: upgrading our cluster tonight
random appreciation post — vllm's continuous batching makes my 4090 feel like a datacenter GPU. doing academic research on a budget and this project is the reason it's possible :pray:
same here. open source LLM serving went from nearly impossible to afternoon-project in like 18 months
the team ships fast. impressive given the complexity of the CUDA code under the hood
anyone using vllm in k8s? curious what your resource request/limit strategy is for GPU pods
we set limits=requests for GPU (nvidia.com/gpu: 1) and leave CPU at 4 cores + 32Gi RAM. no overcommit on GPU
makes sense. do you bother with NUMA pinning?
only for the multi-GPU pods. single GPU is fine without it. the default scheduler usually does the right thing
we use GPU time-slicing on dev nodes to save cost — not recommended for prod but fine for testing

benchmark numbers for anyone curious — llama-3-8b on A100 80GB: ~2800 tok/s throughput at batch size 256 with vllm 0.5.0. comparison: raw HF transformers is about 400 tok/s at batch 8 (can't do 256 without paged attn)
7x speedup is wild. what's the latency profile at batch 256?
TTFT ~800ms, per-token ~3ms at that throughput. for interactive use you'd back off to batch 32 and get TTFT ~200ms
how do I pass a custom chat template to vllm? I have a model with a non-standard instruct format

use `--chat-template /path/to/template.jinja` when starting the server. the template is a Jinja2 file in the same format as HuggingFace's chat_template field in tokenizer_config.json. you can also extract the template from the model's tokenizer_config and save it as a file to start from
worked. I had to tweak the jinja syntax a bit (the model's template used some transformers-specific extensions) but once I cleaned it up vllm accepted it. adding this to the team wiki

production incident question (not urgent, post-mortem): we had vllm OOM-kill after ~6 hours of stable operation. memory grows slowly over time. using llama-2-13b, no quantization, single A100. is there a known memory leak?

there was a KV cache block leak in some 0.3.x versions when requests were cancelled mid-generation (e.g. client disconnects). was it fixed in your version? check if you see a lot of CANCELLED requests in the logs around the time memory starts climbing
lots of cancelled requests, yeah — we have aggressive client timeouts. is there a fix?
patched in 0.3.3. updating should fix it. if you can't update immediately, reduce client timeout aggressiveness — the leak happens when cancel arrives during KV cache prefill
updated to 0.4.0 (skipped 0.3.3, already had it staged). running for 18 hours without the growth. thanks for the context
